Predator-prey dynamics

Source: vignettes/s-predator-prey.Rmd
s-predator-prey.Rmd

Predator-prey dynamics

What it models. Predator-prey coevolution is one of the oldest and most empirically grounded problems in population biology. The classical Lotka-Volterra equations predict sustained oscillations in which predator abundance tracks prey abundance with a characteristic lag (Lotka 1925; Volterra 1928). In clade, predators are autonomous agents with their own energy budgets, movement rules, and reproduction thresholds, so oscillations emerge from individual behaviour rather than being imposed by differential equations.

Key parameters.

Parameter Default Effect
n_predators_init 0 Number of predators at tick 1; 0 disables predators entirely
predator_attack_strength 40.0 Energy damage dealt to prey per successful attack
predator_energy_gain 30.0 Energy gained by predator after a successful attack
predator_min_repro_energy 200.0 Energy threshold for predator reproduction
predator_max_agents 50 Hard cap on predator population size
grass_rate 0.05 Grass regrowth rate; sets prey carrying capacity

Expected output. Watch n_agents (prey) and n_predators over time. At the default parameters predators barely reproduce, so prey simply rise to their grass-imposed carrying capacity and stay there — no interesting dynamics. At the calibrated parameters in the chunk below (discovered via a grid search over predator_energy_gain × predator_min_repro_energy × n_predators_init × grass_rate in dev/audit/fidelity/predator_prey.R), the system produces one clear boom-bust cycle — prey climb to ~460 while predators grow to their cap, then crash at tick ~200 as predation pressure exceeds prey renewal — followed by damped coexistence near a prey equilibrium of ~300. This is the classic signature of spatially-structured Lotka-Volterra dynamics: the first cycle is visible, but sustained oscillations are damped by local refugia (Huffaker 1958; Comins & Hassell 1996; Murdoch et al. 1992).

library(clade)
library(ggplot2)

s <- default_specs()
s$n_predators_init          <- 10L
s$n_agents_init             <- 100L
s$grid_rows                 <- 30L
s$grid_cols                 <- 30L
s$predator_energy_gain      <- 30
s$predator_min_repro_energy <- 50
s$predator_max_agents       <- 100L
s$grass_rate                <- 0.20
s$max_ticks                 <- 1000L
s$random_seed               <- 42L

env  <- run_alife(s)
data <- get_run_data(env)

ggplot(data$ticks, aes(x = t)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = n_agents,    colour = "Prey"),     linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_line(aes(y = n_predators, colour = "Predators"), linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c("Prey" = "#2196F3", "Predators" = "#F44336"),
    name   = NULL
  ) +
  labs(
    x     = "Tick",
    y     = "Population size",
    title = "Predator-prey: boom-bust cycle at calibrated parameters"
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 12)

Calibrated predator-prey regime (seed 42, 1000 ticks). Predators saturate at their cap by tick 30; prey climb to ~460 by tick 200, crash to ~250 as predator pressure builds, then settle into damped oscillations near 300. One clear boom-bust cycle — sustained LV oscillations are damped by spatial refugia in the agent-based model.

What we found. At the calibrated parameters shown above, seed 42, 1000 ticks: predators grow from 10 to the hard cap of 100 by tick ~30 as prey provide abundant energy; prey climb from 150 to a peak of 466 by tick 200; prey crash to 245 over ticks 200–250 as predator pressure exceeds prey renewal; the population then settles into damped oscillations around a mean of ~300 prey with amplitude ±30 for the rest of the run. Across 10 seeds (dev/audit/fidelity/predator_prey.R), the oscillation score — the magnitude of the most negative autocorrelation in the lag-20-to-lag-100 range after tick 100 — is 0.39 ± 0.14, confirming a reproducible (but damped) cycle. Sustained LV oscillations comparable to Lotka’s 1925 mean-field derivation are not reproduced here: the spatial ABM dampens them via local prey refugia, consistent with Huffaker (1958) and Comins & Hassell (1996). The prior vignette claimed a classical 30-tick LV lag, which is not supported by this audit; the first crash lag is ~50 ticks and subsequent cycles are not strongly periodic. Companion fidelity report: dev/audit/fidelity/predator_prey.md.

Discovery experiments

The calibrated regime reproduces one boom-bust cycle + damped coexistence. Three open questions for going beyond:

  1. Can sustained LV oscillations be recovered? Huffaker (1958) achieved sustained oscillations in a spatial mite system only by breaking the grid into patches with dispersal barriers. Try lowering predator_max_agents from 100 back to 50 or 25, reducing predator_sense_radius to sharpen the patch scale, and raising grid_rows/grid_cols so local refugia persist. Measure the oscillation score across a grid-size sweep: does score rise with area?

  2. Prey trait evolution under predation. Turn on body_size_evolution = TRUE. Under predation, are larger prey selected for (harder to kill) or smaller prey (faster maturation)? Run 10 seeds each at 0, 5, and 15 predators for 1000+ ticks and compare final mean_body_size.

  3. Predator-side calibration: what makes predator_attack_strength matter? The vignette’s original “CMA-ES calibrated regime” with predator_attack_strength = 1 and grass_rate = 4.22e-06 was a numerical-optimisation artefact, not biology. A proper sweep of predator_attack_strength in the [10, 120] range at the calibrated baseline above would map the hunting-efficiency dose-response curve.
